title: Networking for DevOps: The Complete A–Z Guide
description: A comprehensive end-to-end guide to networking for DevOps engineers. Covers OSI model, TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, load balancing, container networking (Docker & Kubernetes), cloud VPC design, troubleshooting tools, monitoring, infrastructure as code, automation, security, and production best practices.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: Networking for DevOps architecture diagram showing network core, cloud VPC, Kubernetes, Docker, load balancer, CDN, monitoring, automation, and security layers

sections.title: Introduction to Networking for DevOps
sections.description: Why every DevOps engineer must understand networking.

items.label: Why Networking Matters in DevOps
items.text:
Networking is the backbone of modern infrastructure. As a DevOps engineer, you design, deploy, and troubleshoot the network layer that connects containers, microservices, cloud resources, and end users. Understanding networking fundamentals is essential for:

- Debugging connectivity issues between services (cURL timeouts, connection refused errors)
- Designing secure cloud architectures (VPCs, subnets, security groups)
- Configuring load balancers and reverse proxies (NGINX, HAProxy, ALB/NLB)
- Managing container networking (Docker bridge, Kubernetes CNI, service meshes)
- Automating infrastructure with Terraform, Ansible, and CloudFormation
- Monitoring and optimizing network performance (latency, throughput, packet loss)
- Implementing zero-trust security models and network policies

This guide covers everything from the OSI model to Kubernetes networking to cloud VPC design — all with practical commands and real-world DevOps scenarios.

items.label: DevOps Networking Stack Overview
text: Layer 1–2 (Physical/Data Link): Ethernet, MAC addresses, VLANs, ARP
Layer 3 (Network): IP addressing, CIDR, routing, VPC peering
Layer 4 (Transport): TCP/UDP, ports, connection states, load balancing
Layer 5–7 (Application): DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, TLS, gRPC, WebSocket
Container Networking: CNI plugins, Overlay networks, Service Mesh
Cloud Networking: VPC, Subnets, Route Tables, IGW, NGW, VPC Peering, Transit Gateway
Monitoring: NetFlow, sFlow, Prometheus node_exporter, Grafana

sections.title: OSI Model & TCP/IP Stack
sections.description: The foundational models that describe how data travels across networks.

rows: HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, SSH, FTP, SMTP, gRPC
API design, web servers, DNS config
Port config, load balancing, firewall rules
MAC addresses, switch config, VLAN tagging

items.label: TCP/IP Model (4 Layers)
items.text:
The TCP/IP model is the practical implementation used on the internet. It maps to the OSI model but is simpler with 4 layers:

1. Application Layer (OSI 5–7): HTTP, DNS, SSH, TLS
2. Transport Layer (OSI 4): TCP, UDP
3. Internet Layer (OSI 3): IP, ICMP, ARP
4. Network Access Layer (OSI 1–2): Ethernet, Wi-Fi, physical

items.label: Data Encapsulation Process
items.code:
# Data encapsulation as it moves down the stack:
# Application Layer:
  HTTP Request: GET /api/users HTTP/1.1
  Headers: Host: example.com, User-Agent: curl/7.68

# Transport Layer (TCP):
  Source Port: 54321
  Destination Port: 443
  Sequence Number: 12345
  TCP Flags: SYN=0, ACK=1

# Internet Layer (IP):
  Source IP: 192.168.1.100
  Destination IP: 93.184.216.34
  TTL: 64, Protocol: TCP (6)

# Network Access Layer (Ethernet):
  Source MAC: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
  Destination MAC: 11:22:33:44:55:66
  Ethernet Type: IPv4 (0x0800)

# View encapsulation with tcpdump:
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -nn -X -c 1 host example.com
12:34:56.789012 IP 192.168.1.100.54321 > 93.184.216.34.443:
  Flags [S], seq 12345, win 65535, options [mss 1460]
          0x0000:  4500 003c 1c46 4000 4006 0000 c0a8 0164  E..<.F@.@......d
          0x0010:  5db8 d822 d431 01bb 3039 0000 0000 0000  ]..".1..09......

sections.title: IP Addressing & Subnetting
sections.description: IPv4, IPv6, CIDR notation, and subnet design for cloud infrastructure.

items.label: Subnetting for Cloud VPC Design
items.code:
# AWS VPC subnet design example:
# VPC: 10.0.0.0/16 (65,536 addresses)

# Public subnets (with Internet Gateway):
Public Subnet 1 (AZ-A):  10.0.1.0/24   (254 hosts)
Public Subnet 2 (AZ-B):  10.0.2.0/24   (254 hosts)
Public Subnet 3 (AZ-C):  10.0.3.0/24   (254 hosts)

# Private subnets (no direct internet access):
Private Subnet 1 (AZ-A): 10.0.10.0/24  (254 hosts)
Private Subnet 2 (AZ-B): 10.0.11.0/24  (254 hosts)
Private Subnet 3 (AZ-C): 10.0.12.0/24  (254 hosts)

# Database subnets (restricted access):
DB Subnet 1 (AZ-A):      10.0.20.0/24  (254 hosts)
DB Subnet 2 (AZ-B):      10.0.21.0/24  (254 hosts)
DB Subnet 3 (AZ-C):      10.0.22.0/24  (254 hosts)

items.label: IPv6 Basics for DevOps
items.code:
# IPv6: 128 bits, hexadecimal notation
# Example: 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334

# Shorthand: leading zeros omitted, :: for zero blocks
# 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334
# = 2001:db8:85a3::8a2e:370:7334

# Common IPv6 prefixes for cloud:
# /64 = standard subnet size
# /56 = typical AWS VPC allocation (256 subnets)
# /48 = large allocation (65,536 subnets)

# Test IPv6 connectivity:
user@host:~$ curl -6 https://api64.ipify.org
2600:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx::xxxx

items.label: IP Address Tools
items.code:
# Check local IPs:
user@host:~$ ip addr show
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500
    inet 10.0.1.42/24 brd 10.0.1.255 scope global eth0

# Public IP:
user@host:~$ curl -s ifconfig.me
203.0.113.42

sections.title: DNS — Domain Name System
sections.description: How DNS resolution works and essential commands for troubleshooting.

items.label: DNS Resolution Process
items.text:
When you type 'example.com' in a browser, here's what happens:

1. Browser checks its local DNS cache
2. OS checks /etc/hosts file
3. Query sent to configured DNS resolver (e.g., 8.8.8.8)
4. Resolver queries Root DNS servers for .com TLD servers
5. .com TLD servers return authoritative nameservers for example.com
6. Authoritative nameservers return the A/AAAA record (IP address)
7. Response is cached and returned to the client

When debugging DNS issues in DevOps:
- Always check cache first (nscd, systemd-resolved, dnsmasq)
- Propagation can take hours due to TTL caches
- Use dig +trace to see the full resolution path

items.label: dig Command (DNS Swiss Army Knife)
items.code:
# Basic A record lookup:
user@host:~$ dig example.com

; <<>> DiG 9.18 <<>> example.com
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 12345
;; flags: qr rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1, AUTHORITY: 2, ADDITIONAL: 1

;; ANSWER SECTION:
example.com.		3600	IN	A	93.184.216.34

;; Query time: 23 msec
;; SERVER: 8.8.8.8#53(8.8.8.8)

# Short answer:
user@host:~$ dig +short example.com
93.184.216.34

# Trace full resolution path:
user@host:~$ dig +trace example.com

# Query specific record types:
user@host:~$ dig mx example.com
user@host:~$ dig txt example.com
user@host:~$ dig ns example.com

# Reverse DNS:
user@host:~$ dig -x 93.184.216.34

items.label: DNS Troubleshooting Commands
items.code:
# nslookup:
user@host:~$ nslookup example.com
Server:		8.8.8.8
Address:	8.8.8.8#53
Non-authoritative answer:
Name:	example.com
Address: 93.184.216.34

# host command:
user@host:~$ host example.com
example.com has address 93.184.216.34

# Check /etc/hosts:
user@host:~$ cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1 localhost
127.0.1.1 my-server
10.0.1.50  db-primary.internal

# Check system DNS resolver:
user@host:~$ resolvectl status
Global
       Protocols: -LLMNR -mDNS -DNSOverTLS DNSSEC=no
Link 2 (eth0)
Current DNS Server: 10.0.0.2
       DNS Servers: 10.0.0.2 8.8.8.8

sections.title: HTTP/HTTPS Protocol Deep Dive
sections.description: Understanding HTTP methods, status codes, headers, and the TLS handshake.

items.label: HTTP Methods & When to Use Them
cURL — The DevOps HTTP Swiss Army Knife
items.code:
# Basic GET:
user@host:~$ curl https://api.github.com/repos/nginx/nginx

# Show response headers:
user@host:~$ curl -I https://example.com
HTTP/2 200
server: nginx/1.24.0
content-type: text/html
content-length: 615

# Verbose output (TLS handshake, DNS, timing):
user@host:~$ curl -v https://example.com
*   Trying 93.184.216.34:443...
* Connected to example.com (93.184.216.34) port 443 (#0)
* ALPN: offers h2,http/1.1
* SSL connection using TLSv1.3 / CHACHA20-POLY1305-SHA256
* ALPN: server accepted h2
> GET / HTTP/2
> Host: example.com
> user-agent: curl/7.68.0
>
< HTTP/2 200
< server: nginx/1.24.0

# POST with JSON body:
user@host:~$ curl -X POST https://api.example.com/users \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer token123" \
  -d '{"name":"Alice","email":"alice@example.com"}'

# Measure timing breakdown:
user@host:~$ curl -o /dev/null -s -w '
  DNS: %{time_namelookup}s
  TCP Connect: %{time_connect}s
  TLS Handshake: %{time_appconnect}s
  TTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s
  Total: %{time_total}s
' https://example.com
DNS: 0.023s
TCP Connect: 0.045s
TLS Handshake: 0.089s
TTFB: 0.134s
Total: 0.234s

items.label: TLS Handshake & Certificate Inspection
items.code:
# Inspect TLS certificates:
user@host:~$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com
CONNECTED(00000003)
depth=2 C=US; O=Internet Security Research Group; CN=ISRG Root X1
verify return:1
depth=1 C=US; O=Let's Encrypt; CN=R3
verify return:1
depth=0 CN=example.com
verify return:1

Server certificate
subject=CN=example.com
issuer=C=US; O=Let's Encrypt; CN=R3

SSL handshake has read 3421 bytes and written 397 bytes
New, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384

# Check certificate expiry:
user@host:~$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
notBefore=May 19 00:00:00 2026 GMT
notAfter=Aug 17 23:59:59 2026 GMT

sections.title: TCP vs UDP
sections.description: Understanding transport layer protocols and when to use each.

items.label: TCP 3-Way Handshake & Connection States
items.code:
# TCP 3-way handshake:
# 1. Client -> Server: SYN (seq=x)
# 2. Server -> Client: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
# 3. Client -> Server: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
# Connection established!

# View handshake with tcpdump:
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -nn -i any host example.com and port 443
13:42:01.123456 IP 10.0.1.42.54321 > 93.184.216.34.443:
  Flags [S], seq 1000, win 65535, options [mss 1460]
13:42:01.123789 IP 93.184.216.34.443 > 10.0.1.42.54321:
  Flags [S.], seq 2000, ack 1001, win 65535, options [mss 1460]
13:42:01.123890 IP 10.0.1.42.54321 > 93.184.216.34.443:
  Flags [.], ack 2001, win 65535

# TCP connection states:
user@host:~$ ss -tn | head -10
State       Recv-Q Send-Q  Local Address:Port   Peer Address:Port
ESTAB       0      0       10.0.1.42:54321      93.184.216.34:443
ESTAB       0      0       10.0.1.42:22         192.168.1.50:40000
TIME_WAIT   0      0       10.0.1.42:44444      203.0.113.42:80
LISTEN      0      128     0.0.0.0:22           0.0.0.0:*

items.label: UDP in DevOps Context
items.code:
# UDP powers critical infrastructure protocols:

# DNS queries (port 53 UDP):
user@host:~$ dig +short example.com @8.8.8.8
93.184.216.34

# DHCP (ports 67/68 UDP):
user@host:~$ sudo dhclient eth0

# NTP time sync (port 123 UDP):
user@host:~$ timedatectl timesync-status
       Server: 10.0.0.2 (ntp.example.com)

# View UDP connections:
user@host:~$ ss -un
State       Recv-Q Send-Q  Local Address:Port   Peer Address:Port
ESTAB       0      0       10.0.1.42:54321      8.8.8.8:53

# Measure UDP throughput:
# Server:
user@server:~$ iperf3 -s -p 5201
# Client:
user@client:~$ iperf3 -c 10.0.1.42 -u -b 100m -t 30
[ ID] Interval           Transfer     Bitrate         Total Datagrams
[  5]   0.00-30.00  sec   358 MBytes   100 Mbits/sec  45875
[  5] Sent 45875 datagrams, 0% loss

sections.title: Ports & Sockets
sections.description: Understanding port numbers, socket states, and how services listen.

items.label: Socket State Inspection
items.code:
# List all listening ports:
user@host:~$ ss -tlnp
State    Recv-Q   Send-Q     Local Address:Port     Peer Address:Port   Process
LISTEN   0        128              0.0.0.0:22            0.0.0.0:*       users:(("sshd",pid=1234,fd=3))
LISTEN   0        511              0.0.0.0:80            0.0.0.0:*       users:(("nginx",pid=2345,fd=8))
LISTEN   0        511              0.0.0.0:443           0.0.0.0:*       users:(("nginx",pid=2345,fd=9))
LISTEN   0        128            127.0.0.1:3000          0.0.0.0:*       users:(("node",pid=3456,fd=21))
LISTEN   0        64                    *:3306                *:*       users:(("mysqld",pid=4567,fd=25))

# Check a specific port:
user@host:~$ ss -tlnp | grep ':5432'
LISTEN   0        128              0.0.0.0:5432          0.0.0.0:*       users:(("postgres",pid=6789,fd=11))

# Check port with fuser:
user@host:~$ sudo fuser 80/tcp
80/tcp:               2345

items.label: Checking Port Connectivity
items.code:
# Quick TCP port check with nc:
user@host:~$ nc -zv google.com 443
Connection to google.com (142.250.80.46) 443 port [tcp/https] succeeded!

# With timeout:
user@host:~$ nc -zv -w 3 10.0.1.50 3306
nc: connect to 10.0.1.50 port 3306 (tcp) timed out

# Check multiple ports:
user@host:~$ for port in 80 443 3000 9090; do
  nc -zv localhost $port 2>&1 | grep -E 'succeeded|open'
done

# UDP test:
user@host:~$ nc -zuv 8.8.8.8 53
Connection to 8.8.8.8 53 port [udp/domain] succeeded!

# Port scan with nmap:
user@host:~$ nmap -p 1-10000 10.0.1.50
Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org )
Nmap scan report for 10.0.1.50
PORT     STATE    SERVICE
22/tcp   open     ssh
80/tcp   open     http
443/tcp  open     https
3306/tcp filtered mysql

sections.title: Network Address Translation (NAT)
sections.description: How NAT works and its role in cloud networking.

rows: Changes source IP of outbound packets
Private instances accessing internet via NAT Gateway
Changes destination IP of inbound packets
Many private IPs to one public IP using different ports
Home router / cloud NAT Gateway (most common)

items.label: NAT in Cloud (AWS NAT Gateway)
items.code:
# AWS VPC NAT Gateway:
# - Managed service in public subnet
# - Allows private subnet instances to access internet
# - Cost: ~$0.045/hour + $0.045/GB data processed

# Terraform configuration:
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  domain = "vpc"
}

resource "aws_nat_gateway" "main" {
  allocation_id = aws_eip.nat.id
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[0].id
  tags = { Name = "main-nat-gateway" }
}

resource "aws_route" "private_nat" {
  route_table_id         = aws_route_table.private.id
  destination_cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
  nat_gateway_id         = aws_nat_gateway.main.id
}

# Verify NAT is working:
user@host:~$ curl -s ifconfig.me
203.0.113.42  # NAT Gateway's public IP

# iptables SNAT example:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j SNAT --to-source 203.0.113.42
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE

items.label: Port Forwarding (DNAT)
items.code:
# iptables port forwarding:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 8080 -j REDIRECT --to-port 80
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 443 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.0.1.50:443

# Enable IP forwarding:
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
user@host:~$ echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf

# Kubernetes port forwarding:
user@host:~$ kubectl port-forward svc/my-service 8080:80
Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8080 -> 80

# Docker port mapping:
user@host:~$ docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx

# SSH local port forwarding:
user@host:~$ ssh -L 9000:localhost:3000 user@jumphost
# http://localhost:9000 forwards through jumphost to remote:3000

sections.title: Firewalls & Security Groups
sections.description: Network security with cloud security groups, NACLs, and iptables.

rows: Deny all inbound, allow all outbound

items.label: Cloud Security Group (Terraform)
iptables for DevOps
items.code:
# Check current rules:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -L -n -v

# Default policies:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -P INPUT DROP
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -P FORWARD DROP
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT

# Allow established connections:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# Allow specific services:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

# Allow loopback:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT

# Rate limit SSH (prevent brute force):
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --set
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -m recent --update --seconds 60 --hitcount 4 -j DROP

# Save rules:
user@host:~$ sudo iptables-save > /etc/iptables/rules.v4

sections.title: Load Balancing Concepts
sections.description: Load balancing algorithms, types, and cloud LB services.

rows: Routes based on HTTP headers, path, host
Routes based on IP:port, ultra-low latency

items.label: Load Balancing Algorithms
Health Checks & Target Groups

sections.title: Container Networking (Docker)
sections.description: Docker networking modes, port mapping, and multi-host networking.

rows: Default network for containers on same host
Containers get own MAC/IP on physical network

items.label: Docker Bridge Networking
items.code:
# List Docker networks:
user@host:~$ docker network ls
NETWORK ID     NAME      DRIVER    SCOPE
cdef12345678   bridge    bridge    local
abcdef123456   host      host      local

# Inspect bridge network:
user@host:~$ docker network inspect bridge
[{
    "Name": "bridge",
    "Driver": "bridge",
    "IPAM": {"Config": [{"Subnet": "172.17.0.0/16", "Gateway": "172.17.0.1"}]}
}]

# Create custom bridge network:
user@host:~$ docker network create --driver bridge --subnet 10.10.0.0/16 my-network

# Run containers on custom network:
user@host:~$ docker run -d --name app --network my-network my-app:latest
user@host:~$ docker run -d --name db --network my-network postgres:16

# DNS resolution by container name:
user@host:~$ docker exec app ping db
PING db (10.10.0.3) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from db.my-network (10.10.0.3): icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.123ms

# Port mapping:
user@host:~$ docker run -d -p 8080:80 -p 8443:443 nginx

items.label: Docker Overlay Networking (Swarm)
items.code:
# Initialize Docker Swarm:
user@host:~$ docker swarm init --advertise-addr 10.0.1.10

# Create overlay network:
user@host:~$ docker network create --driver overlay --attachable my-overlay

# Deploy service on overlay:
user@host:~$ docker service create --name web --network my-overlay --replicas 3 nginx

# Verify overlay:
user@host:~$ docker network inspect my-overlay
[{
    "Name": "my-overlay",
    "Driver": "overlay",
    "IPAM": {"Config": [{"Subnet": "10.0.0.0/24", "Gateway": "10.0.0.1"}]}
}]

# Docker DNS resolution across nodes:
user@host:~$ docker exec container1 sh -c 'getent hosts web'
10.0.0.2  web
10.0.0.3  web

sections.title: Kubernetes Networking
sections.description: CNI plugins, Services, Ingress, Network Policies, and Service Mesh.

items.label: Kubernetes Networking Model
items.text:
Kubernetes has strict networking requirements:

1. Pods can communicate with all other Pods without NAT (flat network)
2. Nodes can communicate with all Pods without NAT
3. Agents (kubelet) see the Pod's IP as the source IP

This is achieved through CNI (Container Network Interface) plugins:
- Calico: Network policy enforcement, BGP routing, high performance
- Cilium: eBPF-based, advanced observability with Hubble
- Flannel: Simple overlay (VXLAN), no network policies
- Weave Net: Mesh overlay with encryption
- Amazon VPC CNI: Native AWS VPC integration (each pod gets a VPC IP)

rows: Points a K8s service to an external DNS name
StatefulSets, each pod needs stable DNS name

items.label: Kubernetes Services & Ingress
items.code:
# ClusterIP Service:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 3000
    protocol: TCP
  type: ClusterIP

# DNS resolution inside cluster:
user@host:~$ kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- nslookup my-app
Server:    10.96.0.10
Name:      my-app
Address 1: 10.100.200.10 my-app.default.svc.cluster.local

# NodePort Service:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-app-nodeport
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: my-app
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 3000
    nodePort: 30080

# Access: curl http://10.0.1.10:30080

# Ingress (requires Ingress Controller):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: my-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - app.example.com
    secretName: app-tls
  rules:
  - host: app.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-service
            port:
              number: 80
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-service
            port:
              number: 80

items.label: Kubernetes Network Policies
items.code:
# Deny all ingress traffic:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress

# Allow only from specific pods:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-api-only
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: database
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: api-server
    ports:
    - port: 5432

# Egress policy (only allow DNS):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-dns-only
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: secure-app
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector: {}
      podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          k8s-app: kube-dns
    ports:
    - port: 53
      protocol: UDP

items.label: Service Mesh (Istio / Linkerd)
items.code:
# Service mesh adds a sidecar proxy to each pod:
# - mTLS between services
# - Traffic splitting (canary deployments)
# - Circuit breaking, retries, timeouts
# - Distributed tracing

# Istio VirtualService (traffic routing):
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  hosts:
  - my-app
  http:
  - match:
    - headers:
        version:
          exact: v2
    route:
    - destination:
        host: my-app
        subset: v2
      weight: 100
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: my-app
        subset: v1
      weight: 100

# Istio DestinationRule:
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: DestinationRule
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  host: my-app
  subsets:
  - name: v1
    labels:
      version: v1
  - name: v2
    labels:
      version: v2

# Check Istio proxy status:
user@host:~$ istioctl proxy-status
NAME              STATUS
my-app-v1-abc     SYNCED

sections.title: Cloud Networking (VPC Design)
sections.description: Designing secure, scalable network architectures on AWS.

rows: Segment of VPC in one AZ

items.label: VPC Design Patterns (Terraform)
VPC Peering & Transit Gateway
VPC Endpoints (PrivateLink)
items.code:
# Gateway Endpoint (S3 - free):
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3" {
  vpc_id       = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name = "com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3"
  route_table_ids = [aws_route_table.private.id]
}

# Interface Endpoint (ECR, etc. - paid):
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id             = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name       = "com.amazonaws.us-east-1.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type  = "Interface"
  subnet_ids         = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

# Test endpoint connectivity:
user@host:~$ aws s3 ls --region us-east-1  # Works via Gateway Endpoint

sections.title: Network Troubleshooting Tools
sections.description: Essential commands for diagnosing network issues.

items.label: ping & ICMP
items.code:
# Basic connectivity test:
user@host:~$ ping -c 4 google.com
PING google.com (142.250.80.46) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 142.250.80.46: icmp_seq=1 ttl=118 time=0.834 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.80.46: icmp_seq=2 ttl=118 time=0.792 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.80.46: icmp_seq=3 ttl=118 time=0.812 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.80.46: icmp_seq=4 ttl=118 time=0.845 ms
--- google.com ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% packet loss, time 3004ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 0.792/0.821/0.845/0.019 ms

# Check MTU:
user@host:~$ ping -M do -s 1472 google.com

# Note: Many cloud providers block ICMP, use nc or curl as alternative

items.label: tcpdump — Packet Analysis
items.code:
# Capture HTTP traffic on port 80:
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -i any -nn port 80

# Capture specific host:
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -i any -nn host 10.0.1.50

# Save to file (for Wireshark analysis):
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -i any -nn -w /tmp/capture.pcap port 443

# Read from capture file:
user@host:~$ tcpdump -r /tmp/capture.pcap -nn

# Capture packet contents (ASCII):
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -i any -nn -A port 80

# Capture DNS queries:
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -i any -nn port 53

# Filter by TCP flags (SYN packets):
user@host:~$ sudo tcpdump -i any 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0'

items.label: iperf3 — Bandwidth Testing
items.code:
# Install iperf3:
user@host:~$ sudo apt install iperf3 -y

# Start server:
user@host:~$ iperf3 -s -p 5201

# Run client (30 second test):
user@host:~$ iperf3 -c 10.0.1.50 -t 30
[ ID] Interval           Transfer     Bitrate         Retr
[  5]   0.00-30.00  sec  3.45 GBytes   988 Mbits/sec   12   sender
[  5]   0.00-30.00  sec  3.45 GBytes   988 Mbits/sec         receiver

# UDP test:
user@host:~$ iperf3 -c 10.0.1.50 -u -b 100m -t 30
[ ID] Interval           Transfer     Bitrate         Total Datagrams
[  5]   0.00-30.00  sec   358 MBytes   100 Mbits/sec  45875
[  5]   0.00-30.00  sec   358 MBytes   100 Mbits/sec   0/45875 (0%)

items.label: Additional Diagnostic Commands
items.code:
# arp - MAC address table:
user@host:~$ arp -n
Address                  HWtype  HWaddress           Iface
10.0.0.1                 ether   aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff   eth0

# ip route - routing table:
user@host:~$ ip route show
default via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0
10.0.0.0/16 dev eth0 proto kernel scope link src 10.0.1.42
172.17.0.0/16 dev docker0 proto kernel scope link src 172.17.0.1

# ethtool - NIC info:
user@host:~$ ethtool eth0
Settings for eth0:
    Speed: 10000Mb/s
    Duplex: Full

# ss - socket statistics summary:
user@host:~$ ss -s
Total: 342 (kernel 456)
TCP:   86 (estab 42, closed 12, orphaned 0, timewait 8/0), ports 0

sections.title: CDN & Edge Networking
sections.description: Content Delivery Networks and global traffic optimization.

items.label: How CDNs Work
items.text:
A CDN (Content Delivery Network) distributes content across a global network of edge servers. When a user requests content:

1. DNS resolves to the nearest CDN edge server (via GeoDNS / Anycast)
2. Edge server serves cached content (cache hit) or fetches from origin (cache miss)
3. Subsequent requests from nearby users get cache hits (faster response)

Key CDN features:
- Caching: Static assets cached at edge with configurable TTL
- DDoS Protection: Absorb large volumetric attacks across global network
- SSL/TLS: Free certificates, end-to-end encryption
- Edge Computing: Lambda@Edge, CloudFront Functions, Cloudflare Workers
- WAF: Web Application Firewall at the edge

Popular CDNs: CloudFront (AWS), Cloudflare, Fastly, Akamai

items.label: AWS CloudFront Configuration

sections.title: Network Monitoring & Observability
sections.description: Monitoring network health, latency, and traffic patterns.

items.label: Key Network Metrics
items.code:
# 1. Latency (RTT):
user@host:~$ ping -c 100 google.com | tail -1
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 0.792/0.821/0.845/0.019 ms

# 2. Throughput:
user@host:~$ iperf3 -c 10.0.1.50 -t 10 | grep 'receiver'
[  5]   0.00-10.00  sec  1.12 GBytes   962 Mbits/sec   receiver

# 3. Packet loss:
user@host:~$ ping -c 1000 -f google.com | tail -2
1000 packets transmitted, 999 received, 0.1% packet loss

# 4. Connection count:
user@host:~$ ss -tn | wc -l
342

# 5. Connection state breakdown:
user@host:~$ ss -tn | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
  42 ESTAB
   8 TIME_WAIT
   2 LISTEN

# 6. TCP retransmits:
user@host:~$ netstat -s | grep -i retransmit
  12 segments retransmitted

# 7. DNS resolution time:
user@host:~$ dig google.com | grep 'Query time'
;; Query time: 23 msec

items.label: Prometheus Network Metrics
VPC Flow Logs
items.code:
# Enable VPC Flow Logs (Terraform):
resource "aws_flow_log" "main" {
  log_destination      = aws_s3_bucket.flow_logs.arn
  traffic_type         = "ALL"
  vpc_id               = aws_vpc.main.id
}

# Query with Athena:
SELECT * FROM vpc_flow_logs
WHERE dst_port = 22
  AND action = 'REJECT'
ORDER BY timestamp DESC
LIMIT 10;

# Install softflowd (NetFlow):
user@host:~$ sudo apt install softflowd -y
user@host:~$ sudo softflowd -i eth0 -n 10.0.1.100:9995 -T full

# Analyze flows with nfdump:
user@host:~$ nfdump -r /var/log/nfdump/flow.dat -s ip | head -10
Top 10 IP addresses:
 10.0.1.10        /24   1.2 GB  45%
 10.0.1.11        /24   800 MB  30%

sections.title: Infrastructure as Code for Networking
sections.description: Managing network infrastructure with Terraform and Ansible.

items.label: Terraform VPC Module
Ansible for Network Automation
items.code:
# Ansible playbook for network device config:
---
- name: Configure network devices
  hosts: routers
  gather_facts: yes
  vars:
    ntp_servers:
      - 0.pool.ntp.org
      - 1.pool.ntp.org

  tasks:
    - name: Configure hostname
      ios_config:
        lines:
          - hostname {{ inventory_hostname }}

    - name: Configure NTP servers
      ios_config:
        lines:
          - ntp server {{ item }}
      loop: "{{ ntp_servers }}"

    - name: Configure ACL (SSH only from office)
      ios_config:
        lines:
          - ip access-list extended SSH_ACCESS
          - permit tcp 203.0.113.0 0.0.0.255 host {{ inventory_hostname }} eq 22
          - deny tcp any host {{ inventory_hostname }} eq 22
          - line vty 0 4
          - access-class SSH_ACCESS in

    - name: Save config
      ios_command:
        commands:
          - write memory

sections.title: Performance & Latency Optimization
sections.description: Identifying and reducing network latency in distributed systems.

items.label: Understanding Network Latency
items.code:
# Sources of network latency:
# 1. Propagation delay: distance / speed of light in fiber (~200 km/ms)
#    NYC to London (~5500 km): ~27.5ms
#    NYC to Sydney (~16000 km): ~80ms
# 2. Transmission delay: packet size / bandwidth
#    1500 byte MTU / 1 Gbps = 0.012ms
# 3. Processing delay: router/switch processing time
# 4. Queuing delay: packets waiting in buffer

# Measure latency with curl:
user@host:~$ curl -o /dev/null -s -w '
  DNS Lookup: %{time_namelookup}s
  TCP Connect: %{time_connect}s
  TLS Handshake: %{time_appconnect}s
  TTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s
  Total: %{time_total}s
' https://example.com
DNS Lookup: 0.023s
TCP Connect: 0.045s
TLS Handshake: 0.089s
TTFB: 0.134s
Total: 0.234s

items.label: TCP Tuning for Low Latency
items.code:
# Increase TCP buffer sizes:
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.core.rmem_max=16777216
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.core.wmem_max=16777216
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_rmem='4096 87380 16777216'
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_wmem='4096 65536 16777216'

# Enable BBR congestion control (Google's algorithm):
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.core.default_qdisc=fq
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control=bbr
user@host:~$ sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control
net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control = bbr

# Enable TCP fast open:
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_fastopen=3

# Increase TCP backlog:
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -w net.core.somaxconn=65535

# Make permanent:
user@host:~$ sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf << 'EOF'
net.core.rmem_max = 16777216
net.core.wmem_max = 16777216
net.ipv4.tcp_rmem = 4096 87380 16777216
net.ipv4.tcp_wmem = 4096 65536 16777216
net.core.default_qdisc = fq
net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control = bbr
net.ipv4.tcp_fastopen = 3
net.core.somaxconn = 65535
EOF
user@host:~$ sudo sysctl -p

items.label: Latency Optimization Strategies
items.code:
# 1. Use CDN for static content
#    CloudFront: 450+ edge locations globally
#    Cloudflare: 330+ locations

# 2. Choose cloud regions close to users:
#    us-east-1 for US East
#    eu-west-1 for Europe
#    ap-southeast-1 for Asia

# 3. AWS Global Accelerator (Anycast routing):
resource "aws_global_accelerator" "main" {
  name            = "main-accelerator"
  ip_address_type = "IPV4"
  enabled         = true
}

# 4. Connection pooling:
upstream backend {
    server 10.0.1.10:3000;
    keepalive 32;
}

# 5. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC):
#    HTTP/2: multiplexing, header compression
#    HTTP/3: over UDP, 0-RTT, no head-of-line blocking

# 6. Enable caching at every level:
#    Browser cache, CDN cache, NGINX proxy cache, application cache (Redis)

sections.title: Troubleshooting Common Network Issues
sections.description: Practical debugging approach for real-world network problems.

items.label: Systematic Troubleshooting Approach
items.code:
# OSI model troubleshooting framework:

# Step 1: Application layer (Layer 7)
user@host:~$ curl -v http://localhost:3000/health

# Step 2: Transport layer (Layer 4)
user@host:~$ nc -zv localhost 3000

# Step 3: Network layer (Layer 3)
user@host:~$ ping -c 4 10.0.1.50

# Step 4: DNS (critical)
user@host:~$ dig +short service.example.com

# Step 5: Firewall
user@host:~$ sudo iptables -L -n -v | grep DROP

# Diagnostic checklist:
cat << 'CHECKLIST'
[ ] Is the service running? (systemctl status <service>)
[ ] Is the port listening? (ss -tlnp | grep <port>)
[ ] Is the firewall allowing traffic? (iptables -L)
[ ] Is DNS resolving correctly? (dig <hostname>)
[ ] Can you reach the host? (ping <ip>)
[ ] Can you reach the port? (nc -zv <ip> <port>)
[ ] What does the application log say?
[ ] What does the network capture show? (tcpdump)
[ ] Has anything changed recently? (git log)
CHECKLIST

rows: Service not running or wrong port
Start service or verify port config
Check resources, add caching, scale up
Check NAT GW, route tables, security groups

items.label: Real-World Debugging Workflow
items.code:
# Scenario: Users report 502 Bad Gateway on https://api.example.com

# 1. Reproduce:
user@host:~$ curl -I https://api.example.com/health
HTTP/2 502

# 2. Check the load balancer:
user@host:~$ curl -I http://internal-alb-12345.elb.amazonaws.com/health
HTTP/1.1 200 OK  # ALB is healthy

# 3. Check individual backends:
user@host:~$ for i in 10.0.1.10 10.0.1.11 10.0.1.12; do
  echo -n "$i: "; curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}
" http://$i:3000/health
done
10.0.1.10: 200
10.0.1.11: 200
10.0.1.12: 000  # Instance 3 is down!

# 4. Investigate:
user@host:~$ ssh 10.0.1.12
user@host:~$ systemctl status app
Active: failed (Result: exit-code)

user@host:~$ journalctl -u app -n 50 --no-pager
Error: EACCES: permission denied, bind 0.0.0.0:3000

# 5. Fix and verify:
user@host:~$ sudo systemctl restart app
user@host:~$ curl -s http://localhost:3000/health
{"status":"ok"}

sections.title: Best Practices for DevOps Networking
sections.description: Production-proven networking recommendations from real-world DevOps.

items.label: Network Design Principles
text: ✓ Design for failure: Use at least 3 availability zones with proper subnet isolation.
✓ Follow least privilege: Security groups should only allow necessary traffic.
✓ Use private subnets for all workloads that don't need direct internet access.
✓ Defense in depth: Security groups + NACLs + WAF.
✓ Use separate VPCs for different environments (dev, staging, production).
✓ Enable VPC Flow Logs in all VPCs for audit trail.
✓ Reserve CIDR ranges for future expansion.
✓ Document your network architecture (diagrams in NetBox or Draw.io).

items.label: Production Readiness Checklist
items.code:
=== NETWORK SECURITY ===
[ ] Security groups restrict inbound to minimum required ports
[ ] NACLs provide subnet-level defense
[ ] No security groups allow 0.0.0.0/0 on SSH (port 22)
[ ] WAF enabled for web-facing ALBs
[ ] DDoS protection (AWS Shield, Cloudflare) enabled
[ ] Network Policies implemented in Kubernetes

=== HIGH AVAILABILITY ===
[ ] Resources span at least 3 availability zones
[ ] Load balancers configured with health checks
[ ] NAT Gateways in each AZ (not a single one)
[ ] Databases in multi-AZ deployment

=== MONITORING ===
[ ] VPC Flow Logs enabled and sent to central logging
[ ] Network metrics in Prometheus/Grafana
[ ] Latency alerts (p95 > 200ms, p99 > 500ms)
[ ] SSL cert expiry monitoring

=== PERFORMANCE ===
[ ] BBR congestion control enabled
[ ] CDN configured for static content
[ ] HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 enabled
[ ] Connection pooling configured

items.label: Security Best Practices

sections.title: Summary & Next Steps
sections.description: Key takeaways and what to explore next.

items.label: Key Takeaways
text: ✓ Networking is the foundation of DevOps — understanding it is essential for debugging, security, and design.
✓ Use the OSI model as a troubleshooting framework: start from Layer 7 and work down.
✓ DNS is the most common root cause of issues — master dig, nslookup, and /etc/hosts.
✓ TCP handshake analysis with tcpdump reveals connection-level problems instantly.
✓ Cloud networking (VPCs, subnets, security groups) should be managed as code with Terraform.
✓ Container networking (Docker overlay, K8s CNI) is a separate skill — understand it before deploying.
✓ Network policies and security groups are NOT the same as firewalls — use defense in depth.
✓ Monitor key metrics: latency (p95/p99), packet loss, retransmits, and connection states.
✓ BBR congestion control, CDN, HTTP/2, and connection pooling can dramatically improve performance.
✓ Always debug systematically: reproduce → isolate → fix → verify.

items.label: Next Steps
text: 1. Practice subnetting until CIDR calculations are second nature.
2. Deploy a three-tier VPC on AWS with Terraform and test connectivity.
3. Use tcpdump to capture and analyze a full HTTP request/response cycle.
4. Set up a Kubernetes cluster and experiment with Services, Ingress, and Network Policies.
5. Configure Prometheus node_exporter and Grafana to monitor network metrics.
6. Implement mTLS between microservices using Istio or Linkerd.
7. Get certified: AWS Advanced Networking Specialty or CKA (Certified Kubernetes Administrator).
8. Read: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (Kurose & Ross).
